dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The first two years mirror the professional B.Arch and are well-ordered (studios ARCH 195 -> 196 -> 295 -> 296, Construction I/II ARCH 223/224, History I/II ARCH 210/211), but coherence weakens sharply in the upper years: Studio V (ARCH 395) is the last required studio and years three and four dissolve into undifferentiated 'ARCH Elective' and 'Free Elective' rows with no required Structures, ECS, or professional-practice core and no culminating studio.
dimensions.suggestion: Add a required upper-level integrative studio and pull core technical courses (Structures, ECS) into the required sequence rather than leaving the entire junior/senior experience elective-driven.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is internally consistent but shallow (deepestPrereqChain 4), ending at Studio V (ARCH 395) because the deeper studio spine is not required here. All 3 dangling references (missingCoursesSample: GER 200, GER 300, PHYS 102A) are gen-ed placeholders and a physics-lab co-requisite, not genuine architecture breaks.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve the PHYS 102A lab and GER 200/300 placeholder references, and if deeper studios are intended, restore the prerequisite chain into the upper years.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives appear in the provided data; only the plan of study and course descriptions are present. This absence is a real catalog finding, and it matters more here because a pre-professional degree must clearly signal how it differs from the accredited B.Arch.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish explicit program learning outcomes that distinguish this non-licensure pre-professional B.S. from the B.Arch (e.g., breadth, path to an accredited M.Arch).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: The plan reconciles exactly (summedTermCredits 120 = statedTotalCredits 120, creditReconcileDelta 0.0) and terms are evenly loaded at 12-16 credits. The distribution is transparent, but it is heavily weighted toward open electives in the upper years (multiple 'ARCH Elective' plus 'Free Elective' slots per term), which is flexible but under-structured for a design degree.
dimensions.suggestion: Cap or define the free-elective share and convert some open ARCH-elective slots into declared technology/history-theory requirements so the upper-year balance is intentional.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The eight-term plan is clean, with labeled terms and per-term credits, and the shared ARCH descriptions are mostly substantive. The main weakness is the large number of unresolved placeholder rows ('ARCH Elective', 'Free Elective', GER lines with null credits) that make the upper years read as a template rather than a plan.
dimensions.suggestion: Annotate each elective slot with its category and credit rule (e.g., '300+ ARCH Technology elective, 3 cr') so placeholder rows are self-explanatory.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: As a non-licensure pre-professional degree the flexibility is defensible, but making Structures, ECS, and Professional Practice optional means graduates can complete the major without core technical or practice currency, and the only required computing course is the dated CS 104. Digital tools (BIM ARCH 337, fabrication) and sustainability remain elective.
dimensions.suggestion: Embed a minimum required technology core (one structures, one environmental-systems, one BIM/computational course) so every graduate leaves with current technical fluency.

descriptionQuality.justification: Sampled descriptions are specific for the foundational courses (ARCH 210/211 History, ARCH 223/224 Construction give real topic content), but the continuation studios are thin or boilerplate-contaminated (ARCH 196 'A continuation of ARCH 195. ARCH 2**...', ARCH 296, ARCH 395 'This course is a continuation of ARCH 296.'), and the MATH entries are terse topic lists.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisites are generally explicit, but the retained studio prereqs carry ambiguous compound logic (ARCH 395 'ARCH 224, and (ARCH 296 or ARCH 264), and ARCH 211 or (ARCH 381 and ARCH 382)' mixes precedence), and ARCH 211's 'ARCH 210 or ARCH 251 and ARCH 252' is unparenthesized. Dangling GER/PHYS references are minor.

missingTopics.topic: Required building technology core (structures and environmental systems)
missingTopics.rationale: Structures I/II (ARCH 303/304) and ECS I/II (ARCH 309/314) exist in the catalog but are not required here, so a B.S. graduate can bypass load paths and building energy entirely, a serious gap for any architecture degree.
With only the dated CS 104 required, BIM (ARCH 337) and digital fabrication (ARCH 301/335) remain optional; parametric/Grasshopper modeling that employers expect is absent from the required core.

missingTopics.topic: Sustainability and environmental performance as a required thread
missingTopics.rationale: Sustainability appears only in electives such as ARCH 571 Sustainable City; a degree in architecture should require at least one environmental-performance course given climate-driven practice.
Professional Practice I/II (ARCH 472/475) are not required in this plan, leaving graduates without exposure to project delivery, contracts, or firm operations.
No course addresses code compliance or universal design; even a non-licensure degree should introduce accessibility and life-safety principles.

emergingTrends.rationale: ML-driven form-finding and text-to-image tools are entering studio pedagogy; the Tools and Techniques sequence (ARCH 110/156) should incorporate them and displace the aging CS 104 computing requirement.
Digital electives (ARCH 301/335/337) show the capacity exists; making a parametric/BIM course required would align this flexible degree with contemporary practice.
ARCH 571 Sustainable City demonstrates faculty strength that could anchor a required environmental-performance thread for climate-era practice.
Courses like ARCH 361 Adaptive Paradigms point to responsive/smart-building interest that an elective-heavy degree could formalize as a concentration.
Landscape and Urbanism (ARCH 324) plus urban electives could extend toward GIS/data-driven site analysis, an increasingly expected skill.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program learning outcomes that clearly position this pre-professional B.S. relative to the accredited B.Arch/M.Arch path.

Introduce a required upper-level integrative studio and a required building-technology core (structures, environmental systems, BIM/computational) instead of leaving juniors and seniors on open electives.
Require at least one sustainability/environmental-performance course (e.g., building ECS or ARCH 571) and modernize the dated CS 104 toward computational design.
Convert generic 'ARCH Elective'/'Free Elective' slots into declared categories with credit rules to give the upper-year plan intentional structure.
Resolve the 3 dangling references (GER 200/300, PHYS 102A) and clean the thin/boilerplate continuation-studio descriptions.
